_meta.purpose: Central data bus for Q1 FY26 branch-level analysis. ALL downstream artifacts (QGR packet, branch scorecards, financial analysis, sub-analyses) should READ FROM this file rather than re-extracting from source documents. Quarterly cadence: input the financial/eNPS/operational data into this file ONCE, then every artifact pulls from it.
_meta.architecture_note: What this is: single source of truth for branch metrics. What this is NOT yet: dynamic data store. The QGR packet, FA Presentation, and existing scorecards still have their data hard-coded in HTML — they don't pull from this file at render time. The Q3'26 qgr-packet-builder skill is the eventual rendering engine. Until then, this file is the canonical source for new artifacts (e.g., remaining 8 branch scorecards) and the audit-trail anchor for verification.

workflow.1_input: Each quarter, populate this JSON with: (a) FA Presentation branch data, (b) Mover eNPS aggregated sheet, (c) QGR packet operational metrics (BBE, personnel-budget, etc.), (d) per-branch QGR rocks + AAR (post-QGR).
workflow.2_compute: Pre-compute composite scores + rankings within the JSON. Composite formula: Σ(weight × grade) / 5 × 100. Letter thresholds in _meta.letter_thresholds.
workflow.3_render: Each branch scorecard, the QGR packet personnel section, and any sub-analysis pulls its data from this file. Comparison claims back-referenced to _rankings_q1_26 as proof.
workflow.4_audit: Any 'highest / only / biggest / lowest / tied / above' claim in a render artifact must be backed by a ranking in this file. If the ranking doesn't support the claim, the claim doesn't ship.

_meta.grade_scale_note: v2.1 (2026-04-30): Renamed A→W (Watch) to remove the A-overload — 'A' previously meant Amber metric grade AND top composite letter AND 'appropriate' personnel-budget letter. New scale uses W for Watch. Math also rescaled: SG=5/G=4/W=2/R=1 with divisor 5, so all-Green = 80 (B+ baseline) instead of all-Green = 75 (B+ floor). Restores 'hitting plan = above average' interpretation and gives the bottom of the scale more room (floor is 20% all-Red instead of 25%).
_meta.philosophy_v3: We grow by growing our people and customers — not at their expense. The composite weights this in three buckets: Financial Engine (30%) = TUR + NOI/Truck (the two metrics that ladder to 'profit per truck,' our chosen economic denominator from the OPSP). People + Customer (40%) = Mover eNPS + Customer NPS + Recognition (leading indicators of whether the engine compounds — if the people side erodes, next quarter's results follow). Critical Numbers (30%) = the two quarterly Critical Numbers (Q1: Driver Safety + Personnel-Budget) — tied to the quarterly theme and rotating each quarter; they predict whether this branch can implement and manage change. A branch that hits its plan by burning out crews will see that here, not just in Q2 revenue.

grading_rubric.tur: SG: +5+ pts above target. G: -1 to +4 pts (on-track range, includes recalibrated targets). W: -2 to -5 pts under (or under-target with calibration caveat at original target). R: worse than -5 pts and no calibration relief.
grading_rubric.noi_per_truck: SG: ≥$13K/truck. G: $10K–$13K/truck. W: $0–$10K/truck. R: negative. Planned-loss branches (Tampa, Fort Worth) caveat-flagged but graded honestly — surface in metric drill-down rather than calibrating away.
grading_rubric.rev_noi_to_plan: REFERENCE ONLY (v3.0). Demoted from graded — derivative metric (profit ÷ plan = a hit-the-target check). NOI/Truck is the unit-economic truth that ladders to OPSP. Prior rubric retained for reference: SG: 100%+ to plan. G: 90-99%. W: 75-89%. R: <75% or flip-to-loss.
grading_rubric.customer_nps: SG: target+1 or better. G: target -1.5 to target+1. W: target -1.5 to -4. R: target -4 or worse.
grading_rubric.mover_enps: SG: +30 or more above company avg. G: 0 to +30 above. W: -10 to 0 below. R: more than -10 below (intervention).
grading_rubric.recognition_pct: SG: ≥90%. G: 75-89%. W: 60-74% (per Cameron 4/30: anything below 75% gets a watch flag). R: <60%.
grading_rubric.driver_safety_score: v3.3.7: SG ≥90 AND <10% HR · G ≥80 AND ≤20% HR · W 70-79 OR (≥80 AND >20% HR) · R <70. Per-branch from Mike's Q1 Samsara breakdown sheet.
grading_rubric.personnel_budget_pct: SG: >110% (over plan — exceeded budget reflects demand strength or planned-loss capacity; rewarded as exceeding the goal, not docked). G: 95-110% (on plan — meeting the budget goal). W: 85-94% (under plan, one or two periods below). R: chronic-F (5/5 below 95%). Cameron 5/4 ruling (LOCKED): over-budget alone does NOT dock; branches above 100% are not penalized. Cameron 5/4 follow-up (LOCKED): over plan (>110%) earns SG, not just G — exceeding the staffing-vs-budget plan is a positive read, same as exceeding any other plan. Q1 application: Fort Worth (1.20) and Garland (1.15) lifted to SG. N. Austin (1.05), McKinney (1.05), Dallas (1.02), Tampa (1.06) remain G (within on-plan band 95-110%). Future quarters: this rubric is canonical — do not reintroduce 110-125% Watch band or any framing that 'overstaffed' is a discipline issue.

v32_architecture_archived._decision_log: v3.2 (2026-05-01) reverted from v3.1 Three-Lens to "v3.0 single composite + growth modifier" after Cameron noted Three-Lens diluted Critical Numbers from 30% → 10% of overall and double-counted NPS/eNPS/Recognition across Lens 1 + Lens 3. Simpler architecture: single composite (v3.0 weights, every metric counted once, Critical Numbers properly at 30%) with a separate growth modifier applied to the composite score.

composite.description: 7-metric weighted composite, v3.0 weights — every metric counted exactly once.

growth_modifier.description: Single Revenue YoY % modifier applied to the composite score. Captures trajectory without restructuring the composite math.
growth_modifier.rationale: Discrete tiers (vs continuous) match Cameron-vocabulary categories (Breakout / Strong / Stable / Flat / Slight decline / Declining). Modifiers calibrated so breakout earns ~+10pts, declining loses ~-7pts; W band straddles 0 with neutral and slight-penalty.

v33_architecture.description: v3.3 (2026-05-02) refines v3.2 in three ways: (1) NOI/Truck moves from graded → REFERENCE — single-quarter data insufficient to calibrate ratio-to-plan rubric; show actual + plan + variance, no grade applied; revisit Q3. (2) Sales Target Attainment graded as new metric at 10% weight, pulled from EMMA Mongo CSV per branch team. (3) TUR weight bumped 15% → 20% (absorbs the 5% freed by Sales coming in at 10% rather than NOI/Truck-replacing 15%) — TUR is well-instrumented per branch and structurally important.

strategic_significance.purpose: Frame each branch's operational grade alongside its scale of cash contribution. The composite (v3.3.1) measures 'is this branch operating well?' Strategic significance measures 'how much does this branch matter to the company's cash output?' Read together to surface highest-leverage interventions: high-cash + low-grade = top fix priority; low-cash + high-grade = good operational discipline at a smaller bet.

tier_thresholds_q1_26.Cash Contributor: 8% to 20% of company NOI · meaningful positive contribution
tier_thresholds_q1_26.Cash Neutral: 0% to 8% of company NOI · small positive
tier_thresholds_q1_26.Growth Bet: Negative NOI · designated planned-loss / new market (e.g., Tampa, Fort Worth) · intentional investment, not performance failure
tier_thresholds_q1_26.Underperforming: Negative or near-zero NOI · NOT designated planned-loss · chronic gap not by design

strategic_significance.tier_label_rationale: 5-tier split chosen over 4-tier 'Cash Drag' label per Cameron 5/2: 'I like the cleaner alternative of in investment and underperforming. But I also like growth bet more than in investment and I think the team would take it better.' The split avoids lumping intentional-loss branches (Tampa, Fort Worth) with branches where negative NOI is NOT the plan (Garland). Internal-comms framing: Growth Bet is forward-looking and tied to company strategy; Underperforming is honest about diagnosis without naming-and-shaming a specific branch in the tier label itself.
strategic_significance.investment_designation_basis: Per FA Presentation v2.7 + audit log v3.0 entries: Tampa is new market still ramping; Fort Worth is planned-loss expansion. Garland is NOT designated investment despite recent BM transition cost — its chronic eNPS/growth/People-Analyzer pattern predates the transition.
strategic_significance.methodology_section_commitment: Tier thresholds + investment designation list MUST land in the QGR packet Methodology section as part of the v3.3.x architectural lineage subsection (step 8 of execution sequence). Methodology must show: (1) why we read two parallel lenses instead of one merged grade, (2) the locked tier thresholds and how branches map to them, (3) the double-count swap-trigger commitment, (4) the investment-designation list and basis. Cameron 5/2 confirmation: 'Tier threshold feels right. Need to make sure that gets into methodology.'
strategic_significance.double_count_swap_trigger: If/when NOI/Truck graduates from ungraded reference to graded composite metric (target: Q3'26 after multi-quarter calibration), Strategic Significance MUST switch denominator from NOI share to revenue share to avoid double-counting NOI in both the diagnostic AND the strategic lens. Cameron-flagged 5/2 evening: 'once we grade NOI (after we calibrate) won't that start to double weight that component a bit?' — yes, hence this commitment.
strategic_significance.comparability_with_grade: Strategic Weight does NOT modify the v3.3.1 final grade in any way. It is a parallel display dimension. The cover hero on each scorecard shows the grade prominently AND a Strategic Weight chip beside it. QGR packet shows two parallel rankings (Operational Excellence + Strategic Significance) above the branch scorecards section.

open_questions_q2: Should QGR Rock Performance graduate into the Critical Numbers slot, or stay separate?
Should the growth modifier widen to include NOI YoY + Critical Number trajectory delta starting Q2?
Where should the all-10 branch ranking live — QGR Packet as a new component, separate dashboard, or scorecard navigation hub?
Should branches with planned-loss quarters (Tampa, Fort Worth) get a structural caveat treatment in the grade, or stay graded honestly with caveats?
Should this scoring system feed downstream consequences — bonus tiering, variable pay, BM evaluation?
Should NOI/Truck stay ungraded long-term, or graduate to graded once we have multi-quarter calibration data?
Should weighted-vs-market versions of Conversion + Cancellation become graded inputs, replacing or augmenting Sales Target?
Should there be a velocity (Δ from prior quarter) modifier added to the final grade once we have Q2 data?

CHRONIC-FLAG MODIFIER: Should branches that have been Watch/Red on the same metric for ≥3 consecutive quarters get a compounding penalty? Q1 surfaced Garland as a chronic case — eNPS, growth, People Analyzer all stuck on the same flags multi-quarter, but Q1 single-quarter snapshot doesn't penalize that pattern. The growth modifier captures revenue YoY direction but not 'this branch has been bad on the same axes for 4+ quarters.' Cameron 5/2: 'my issue with Garland is that its velocity is unchanged for so long across a number of their bad things — I guess we will catch that more as quarters progress.' Calibration window: needs ≥3 quarters of data to apply meaningfully.

MARKET-STAGE NORMALIZATION: Should the scorecard normalize for market-stage variables (metro cost structure, brand-pricing-power maturity) the same way it caveats planned-loss for Tampa and Fort Worth? Surfaced by Dallas Q1: 10-year-old branch with A operational grade across all 7 axes but compressed margin reflecting DFW cost structure + brand maturity. Cost-of-living and pricing-power maturity vary by metro and aren't operational levers, but NOI/Truck and margin penalize them. Cameron 5/2: 'dallas is 10 years old. So it may be partly brand presence but also market based.' Would require: (a) per-metro cost-structure baseline, (b) brand-pricing-power maturity score, (c) decision on whether to apply as caveat (current Tampa/FW pattern), modifier (penalty/bonus), or full normalization (subtract structural variance from raw metric).

QGR-PACKET-BUILDER SKILL DESIGN (Q3'26 build target): Q1'26 build pattern was sequential pair-N (build-then-audit per pair, 5 pairs across the session). Cameron 5/2 design note: future skill should accept (branch_ids[], canonical_json_path, canonical_template_html_path, sparse_narrative_principle) and execute three parallel waves: (1) N parallel build-agents (one per branch) — each receives JSON branch object + template + sparse-narrative principle as inputs and writes one scorecard HTML. (2) N parallel audit-agents (one per built scorecard) — each receives built HTML + JSON + _rankings_q1_26 for superlative verification + per-card rubric checks. (3) Main thread batches fixes from structured audit findings. (4) ONE final cross-card consistency agent reads all N + JSON + checks chart-label naming, ranking claims that reference each other, rubric language, and version-tag drift. Estimated: ~10 build + 10 audit + 1 final sweep = 3-5 min total wall-clock vs 30+ min sequential. Cost trade is worth it. Bake the sparse-narrative principle + the most common audit failure modes (false superlatives, lift-math errors at 10%/15% weights, causal inference, cross-card naming) into the audit-agent prompt as a checklist. Q1'26 audit-fix counts per pair: P1=6, P2=7, P3=3, P4=7, P5=3 + final sweep=6 = ~32 fixes total across 10 cards.

narrative_principle_sparse_by_default.rule: Branch-level narrative ships ONLY what data verifies. Speculative causes — even plausible-sounding ones — must be flagged as unverified hypotheses or omitted entirely. The default is sparse, not comprehensive.
narrative_principle_sparse_by_default.why: Cameron 5/2: 'I think we need to leave branch explanations relatively sparse as we build them out so I don't have to come back through and make a bunch of corrections.' Pattern that triggered this: Albert kept layering hypothesized causes (fleet ramp, higher labor, brand maturity) into Dallas narrative without verifying. Each layer required Cameron pushback to correct. Sparse-by-default reverses the pattern — no cause claim ships unverified.
how_to_apply: 1. Lead with verified data (composite grade, raw metrics, source-confirmed comparisons)
2. Name the gap or anomaly cleanly without ascribing cause
3. If a cause hypothesis is necessary for context, label it as 'hypothesis — not yet verified' or 'OPEN'
4. List rejected hypotheses explicitly so future-Albert (or future-reader) doesn't re-litigate them
5. List 'what we do not know' as a first-class field, not buried in a footnote
applies_to: Audit log action descriptions (when summarizing branch state)
narrative_principle_sparse_by_default.exemption: Composite math, ranking math, grading rubric — these are deterministic and don't need this principle applied. Only narrative/cause/explanation language is in scope.

audit_learning_registry.purpose: Catalog of recurring failure modes the Q1'26 audit-agents kept catching. Bake this into the future qgr-packet-builder skill (Q3'26 build target) as the audit-agent prompt checklist. Each entry: pattern_name, what_to_catch, why_it_matters, how_audit_agent_should_handle. Captured 5/2/2026 at end of Pair 5 build.

patterns.what_to_catch: Claims like 'highest in company', 'lowest', 'only branch', 'most severe', 'second-strictest', 'tier-leading' that don't survive cross-branch verification.
patterns.why_it_matters: Pairs 1-4 averaged 4 false-superlative catches per pair. Most common pattern: branch claims 'only branch below target' when 3 are below; 'second-strictest enforcement' when actually third or fifth.
patterns.how_audit_agent_should_handle: For EVERY superlative claim in the scorecard, verify against JSON _rankings_q1_26 before allowing publication. If unsupported, strip or correct.

patterns.what_to_catch: Claims like 'lift to G adds X composite points' where X is wrong because the math wasn't done with the metric's actual weight.
patterns.why_it_matters: R→G lift on 10% weight = 6 composite points; on 15% weight = 9 composite points. Audit agents kept catching '+3 composite points' or '+4.5 composite points' that violated the formula (grade_delta × weight ÷ 5).
patterns.how_audit_agent_should_handle: For every 'lift adds X composite points' claim, verify against (grade_delta × weight ÷ 5) where SG=5, G=4, W=2, R=1. Flag if mismatched.

patterns.what_to_catch: Phrases like 'X drove Y', 'ramping', 'lifting NPS removes the drag', 'carrying full team alone', 'special case', 'cost-cause speculation'.
patterns.why_it_matters: Sparse-narrative principle violations. Cameron 5/2: 'this is you making inferences off data.' Each one requires Cameron pushback to correct — expensive in audit cycles.
patterns.how_audit_agent_should_handle: Reference _meta.narrative_principle_sparse_by_default. Flag any causal verb ('drove', 'caused', 'led to', 'lifting X removes Y', 'carrying') or attribution-claim that isn't directly supported by JSON. Soften to parallel-fact framing or strip.

patterns.what_to_catch: Lines that name another branch in a comparison that isn't necessary to explain THIS card's grade. Examples: 'less severe than Leander/Houston', 'lowest of 10 (next: Leander 87.8)', 'unlike X branch', 'Leander posted F but stayed positive'.
patterns.why_it_matters: Cameron 5/2 (added at session end): public-facing scorecards shouldn't drag other branches into a card's narrative. Neutral ranking ('second-highest after FW') is OK; comparisons that frame another branch unflatteringly are not. Different from false-superlatives — the data may be correct, but the social cost of putting it on this card isn't worth the marginal information.
patterns.how_audit_agent_should_handle: Flag any line where another branch is named in a way that goes beyond neutral ranking. Categories to flag: (a) named comparisons in self-criticism context, (b) 'X group' labels that implicitly name other cards (e.g., 'the chronic-F branches'), (c) 'most severe' / 'less severe than' phrasings that rank within a flagged group. Default action: strip the named-comparison or replace with the JSON's own diagnostic label.

patterns.what_to_catch: References to BM transitions, terminations, who-replaces-whom, performance commentary that names a specific person in a way that could be perceived as calling them out negatively.
patterns.why_it_matters: Cameron 5/2: 'we don't need to mention that the BM was fired. Not anywhere in this document which is public facing.' Even when factually true (e.g., a transition is approved internally), public-facing scorecards should not contain personnel decisions that haven't been publicly announced. Especially: 'Jack inherits', 'Terrell's last quarter', 'X drove the transition decision', 'sole BM activity (BM to model team-wide habit in Q2)' — anything that frames an individual leader's performance as the reason for a personnel decision.
patterns.how_audit_agent_should_handle: Hard-strip ANY mention of: BM transitions, terminations, internal personnel decisions, 'X takes over Q2', 'X inherits', 'last quarter as BM', 'BM exit'. Roster data ('Sales rep X attained Y%') is OK. Performance commentary that singles out a leader ('BM in W band while reps cleared 100%') is NOT — soften to team-level framing without naming the BM as the drag.

patterns.what_to_catch: Architectural-tier labels using English words that have loaded everyday meaning (e.g., 'Underperforming' Strategic Weight tier, 'Cash Drag' tier label, 'chronic-F' pattern label).
patterns.why_it_matters: Cameron 5/2: 'I feel like they aren't the only underperforming branch. Leander got an F right?' The tier label collides with a different reading (operational grade). When the loaded label appears in a callout without disambiguation, the reader trips on the apparent contradiction.
patterns.how_audit_agent_should_handle: When a card APPLIES a loaded-label tier to itself (not just defines it in the rubric), require disambiguation in the same sentence. Example: 'Underperforming Strategic Weight tier' (with the qualifier) instead of just 'Underperforming tier'. Pair with one-sentence definition (e.g., 'meaning lost money on a positive-NOI plan').

patterns.what_to_catch: Chart-script bugs that get copy-pasted from a template scorecard to all other scorecards (e.g., wrong threshold value, mislabeled annotation line, hardcoded branch name in a generic helper).
patterns.why_it_matters: Discovered 5/2 Pair 5 audit: the '−5% R threshold' annotation on the Revenue YoY chart was wrong (rubric says −3%). Bug originated in Fort Worth template script and propagated to ALL 10 scorecards. Per-card audit didn't catch it because every card had the same bug.
patterns.how_audit_agent_should_handle: Final cross-card sweep is non-negotiable for catching propagated template bugs. Audit-agent for the cross-card sweep should explicitly check: chart annotation values, hardcoded branch names in generic helpers, threshold lines in chart scripts. Compare across cards — if all 10 cards have the same chart claim, verify it once against the rubric.

patterns.what_to_catch: Branches over their personnel-budget plan (>100% attainment) being graded W or R because the rubric treats 'overstaffed' as a discipline issue. Specifically: a 110-125% Watch band, 'Overstaffed; scheduling-fit issue' diagnoses, or any framing that over-budget is bad.
patterns.why_it_matters: Cameron 5/4 ruling (LOCKED): over-budget alone does NOT dock. Over plan (>110%) earns SG, not just G. The reasoning: branches above 100% of personnel plan are reflecting demand response or planned-loss capacity — not poor discipline. Demand-side mismatches (low Rev/Truck markets) are tracked separately, not via the Personnel-Budget grade. The original Q1 rubric had a 110-125% Watch band; that band was retired 5/4. Q1 Fort Worth (120%) and Garland (115%) were both lifted from W → SG and their composite + final grades cascaded (FW B 77 → A− 86; Garland C 67 → B 76).
patterns.how_audit_agent_should_handle: For every scorecard, verify Personnel-Budget grade against the v3.4.4 rubric: SG >110% · G 95-110% · W 85-94% · R chronic-F (5/5 below 95%). Flag any scorecard where a >100% branch is graded W or R. Flag any narrative text using 'overstaffed,' 'scheduling-fit issue,' '110% G ceiling,' or 'overstaffing signals discipline issue' as carrying the retired rubric. Verify the methodology section's Personnel-Budget rubric data-source div references v3.4.4 + the Cameron 5/4 ruling.

fix_count_per_pair_history.interpretation: Per-pair audit catches per-card errors. Final cross-card sweep catches template-propagation bugs and cross-card consistency. Post-publish callout pass catches loaded-language and cross-branch-callout patterns the per-card audits accept as factual. All three layers are needed.

must_include_blocks_per_scorecard.purpose: Content blocks that EVERY scorecard must include when built or batched. Each block lists the source-of-truth template + per-branch swap-in instructions. Audit agent should fail any scorecard missing one of these blocks.

blocks.source_template: branch-scorecard-naustin.html — `.composite-math` div inside `section.philosophy`, sitting between the `.philosophy-grid` and `.philosophy-close` paragraph.
blocks.purpose: Demystifies the Composite = Σ(weight × grade points) ÷ 5 formula for non-spreadsheet readers. Walks through max-points = 100 × 5 = 500, divided by 5 → 0–100 scale. Explains why grade scale is SG=5 / G=4 / W=2 / R=1 (skipping 3) so all-Green = 80 baseline.
blocks.per_branch_swap_in: The 'N. Austin worked example' paragraph is the only branch-specific element. For each branch, replace the example with that branch's actual: (a) per-metric weight × grade-points sum, (b) divide by 5 to get composite score, (c) add growth modifier, (d) state final letter grade. Pull values from JSON `_branch_summaries[branch].composite_v33` and the per-metric grades.

reframe_v33_4.qgr_packet_narrative_v33_4: Dallas — Operational A grade across all 7 axes. Q1 NOI/Truck reflects a combination of (a) DFW market cost structure (higher labor, lease, insurance vs Austin) and (b) Dallas-specific brand maturity that doesn't yet support Austin-tier pricing despite the branch being 10 years old — neither factor alone is sufficient explanation. Rev/Move is at parity with Austin (~$1,258) despite Dallas pricing ~$5/hr lower, meaning move-level economics are healthy — the gap is fixed-cost coverage, which compresses in Q1's slow season. Watch summer (Q2/Q3) for margin recovery as volume spreads fixed cost. Continue investing in brand-presence to close the pricing-power gap over time.
reframe_v33_4.fleet_ramp_hypothesis: REJECTED — fleet sheet shows N. Austin and S. Austin added more new trucks in 2025 than Dallas, and gross purchase count alone is insufficient (Austin sold 3-4 trucks last year offsetting). Fleet age profile across Austin + Dallas is comparable; not the explanation.
reframe_v33_4.parity_claim_correction: Council finding 'rev/truck parity with N. Austin' was wrong. Actual: Dallas $61,114 vs N. Austin $78,805 (-22.5%). Source error: Albert's prompt to council framed branches as 'same revenue scale' which they accepted as given.
reframe_v33_4.moves_per_truck_caveat: Dallas 48.6 moves/truck × $1,258 Rev/Move ≠ N. Austin 62.8 × $1,255 in a meaningful operational sense. Comparing Moves/Truck alone is misleading because branches with fewer-but-larger moves (e.g. Leander 41.0 moves × $1,846 Rev/Move) can outperform branches with more-but-smaller moves. The Rev/Truck gap reflects fixed-cost absorption per truck across whatever move volume the branch books, not a productivity ranking.
reframe_v33_4.diagnostic_status: Hypothesis testable in Q2/Q3 — if NOI/Truck climbs into $8K-$12K band as volume spreads fixed cost, combo hypothesis (DFW market + brand maturity) holds. If Q2/Q3 stays compressed, deeper cost-side examination needed.
reframe_v33_4.what_the_data_did_not_show: We do NOT have evidence of a Beattie performance gap. Operational scores are A across all 7 axes. The story is structural (market + brand-pricing-power), not personnel.
reframe_v33_4._status: SUPERSEDED by reframe_v33_5 — v33_4 contained speculative cause language ('higher labor, lease, insurance', 'brand-pricing-power maturity') that Albert had not verified. Cameron 5/2 corrected: 'Dallas pay rates for movers are the same. Maybe they run more OT but not sure. I think the higher labor is just triggering me. I feel like we are making some assumptions without checking.'

reframe_v33_5.qgr_packet_narrative_v33_5: Dallas — Operational A grade across all 7 axes. Q1 NOI/Truck ($1,283) compressed vs Austin sister branches (N. Austin $12,163, S. Austin $11,520) at comparable fleet size. Rev/Move parity (~$1,258 vs N. Austin $1,255) despite Dallas pricing ~$5/hr lower. Mover pay rates are equivalent across markets. Cause analysis OPEN — not yet decomposed. Watch Q2/Q3 for direction.
verified_facts: Operational composite: 93 A across all 7 graded metrics
Q1 NOI/Truck: $1,283 (vs N. Austin $12,163, S. Austin $11,520, McKinney $7,710)
Mover pay rates: equivalent across markets (Cameron-confirmed)
Fleet age profile: comparable to N. Austin (both branches added 2-3 trucks in 2025; Austin sold 3-4 last year per Cameron)
rejected_hypotheses: Fleet ramp explains the gap — REJECTED (fleet age profile comparable; gross purchase count without sells is insufficient)
'Same revenue scale as N. Austin' — REJECTED (Dallas $855K vs N. Austin $1.10M = 29% gap)
Higher mover labor cost in DFW — REJECTED (Cameron-confirmed mover pay rates same)
Council 'rev/truck parity with N. Austin' — REJECTED ($61K vs $79K = -22.5% gap)
unverified_hypotheses: Higher non-labor cost structure (lease, insurance, OT) — speculated by Albert, NOT confirmed by Paul/Cameron
Brand-pricing-power maturity — plausible but Dallas is 10 years old so 'brand maturity' alone is suspect
Q2/Q3 summer volume recovery — directional intuition, not yet data-supported
reframe_v33_5.what_we_do_not_know: We do not know why Dallas margin is compressed. We have not decomposed cost structure with Paul. We have not validated whether DFW market dynamics or Dallas-specific brand factors dominate. We have not confirmed whether OT, lease, insurance, marketing spend, or another cost line is driving the gap. Reporting the gap honestly without a stated cause is correct until decomposed.
reframe_v33_5.what_the_data_did_not_show: No Beattie performance gap. Operational scores are A across all 7 axes. The gap is structural-or-financial, not personnel.

_audit_log.action: Dallas margin framing fully reworked through council session + Cameron pushback + fleet-data validation. FOUR errors corrected (no math impact on grade — Dallas remains 93 A composite + 4.0% NOI share Cash Neutral). (1) Council-input error: Albert's prompt framed N. Austin and Dallas as 'same revenue scale,' which was wrong ($1.10M vs $855K = 29% gap). The Numbers Person took it as given and approximated N. Austin rev/truck at $60K, claiming parity. Actual: Dallas $61,114 vs N. Austin $78,805 (-22.5%). (2) Fleet-ramp hypothesis: Albert's follow-up framing claimed Dallas was 'mid fleet ramp' driving Q1 margin compression. Fleet sheet validation showed N. Austin added 3 new trucks in 2025, S. Austin added 4, Dallas added 2 — Austin branches absorbed MORE new fleet, not less. Cameron flagged additional gap: gross purchase count alone insufficient without netting against trucks sold (Austin sold 3-4 last year). Hypothesis REJECTED. (3) Pricing assumption: Albert claimed 'Rev/Move parity = pricing parity.' Cameron corrected — Dallas charges ~$5/hr less than Austin branches, so Rev/Move parity actually means Dallas is doing larger jobs (more hours/move or larger crews) at lower hourly rate, not equivalent unit pricing. (4) Moves/Truck stand-alone diagnostic: Albert flagged Dallas's 48.6 moves/truck as the 'dominant explanation' for the Rev/Truck gap. Cameron corrected — Moves/Truck must be paired with Rev/Move; Leander at 41.0 moves × $1,846 Rev/Move outperforms branches with more-but-smaller moves. Volume isn't destiny. FINAL REFRAME: Dallas Q1 NOI/Truck reflects combination of (a) DFW market cost structure and (b) brand maturity not yet supporting Austin-tier pricing despite the branch being 10 years old. Neither factor alone is sufficient. Rev/Move parity at lower hourly rate suggests healthy move-level economics; the gap is fixed-cost coverage which compresses in Q1 slow season. Hypothesis testable in Q2/Q3. NOT a Beattie performance story — operational scores are A across all 7 axes.
